1: You shall not spread a false report (lo tissa shav): Nasa' = to lift up/carry; shav = emptiness/falsehood/vanity (the same word as the third commandment's 'in vain' — lo tissa et-shem YHWH lashav). To carry a false report is to do with a neighbor's reputation what the commandment forbids doing with YHWH's name. You shall not join hands with a wicked man to be a malicious witness (ed chamas): Ed chamas = witness of violence/injustice; chamas = violence/wrongdoing (the same term for the corruption that preceded the Flood, Gen 6:11). False witness is an act of violence against the innocent.
2: You shall not follow a majority to do evil (lo-tih'yeh acharei rabim l'ra'ot): Acharei rabim = after the many/majority; the law against mob justice — the majority principle applies to voting majorities in court cases but not to mob action. Nor shall you be partial (tit'neh) to a poor man in his lawsuit: Natal = to incline/be partial; even the poor (dal = thin/weak) receive no automatic benefit of judicial partiality. Justice is not a redistribution mechanism but a truth-seeking process.
3: You shall not be partial to a poor man (dal) in his lawsuit: The prohibition of favoritism toward the poor in court — a surprising law that balances the many laws protecting the poor from oppression. Justice is impartial; poverty does not create automatic legal advantage. Cf. Lev 19:15: 'you shall not be partial to the poor or defer to the great.'
4: If you meet your enemy's (oyivcha) ox or his donkey going astray, you shall bring it back to him: Oyev = enemy (personal adversary, not necessarily a foreign enemy). The return of the enemy's stray animal is the first formal enemy-love law in the Hebrew Bible — predating and forming the backdrop to Jesus's 'love your enemies' (Matt 5:44). The law does not require loving feelings but loving actions: the animal must be returned regardless of the relationship.
5: If you see the donkey of one who hates you (son'acha) lying down under its burden: Sone' = one who hates/is hostile to you; the law extends even to the enemy's animal. You shall refrain from leaving him with it; you shall rescue it with him (azov ta'azov imo): The doubled infinitive absolute (azov ta'azov) is emphatic — you shall certainly help. The phrasing 'with him' (imo) implies joint effort; the law creates a situation of forced cooperation between enemies. Working side by side to rescue an animal is itself a social reconciliation mechanism.
6: You shall not pervert the justice (mishpat) due to your poor (evyoncha) in his lawsuit: Evyon = destitute/poor (a stronger term than dal — the utterly impoverished, with no resources); mishpat = justice/judgment/rule. The legal system must serve the poor; its primary test is whether it protects the powerless. The poor man's case (rib = dispute/case/lawsuit) must be heard on its merits.
7: Keep far from a false charge (davar shav): Shav = emptiness/falsehood (same as v.1); Do not kill the innocent (naqi) and righteous (tzaddiq): Naqi = clean/innocent; tzaddiq = righteous/in the right. For I will not acquit the wicked (rasha'): YHWH's own justice is the model and guarantee for the human court; the divine standard backs the human legal order.
8: And you shall take no bribe (shochad): Shachad = a gift/bribe offered to pervert justice; shochad = the bribe. For a bribe blinds the clear-sighted (piqchim) and subverts the cause of those who are in the right (tzaddiqim): Piqeach = one who sees clearly/is sighted (the opposite of blind); the bribe reverses vision — the seeing become blind, the righteous become perverted. The bribery prohibition is the foundational anti-corruption clause of Israelite jurisprudence.
9: You shall not oppress (tilchatz) a sojourner (ger): Lachatz = to press/oppress/squeeze (related to the Egyptian lachatz in 3:9 — the oppression Israel experienced). You know the heart (nefesh) of a sojourner: The use of nefesh (soul/inner life) for 'the experience/feeling of a foreigner' is striking — Israel is commanded to empathize with the ger's inner life because they know it experientially. The ethical command is grounded in experiential memory, not abstract principle.
10: For six years (shesh shanim) you shall sow your land: The sabbath-year (shemittah) law opens with the positive command: six years of full cultivation. The Sabbath principle extends from the week to the year — creation rest is built into the agricultural calendar. Israel's economy must breathe.
11: But in the seventh year (hashevii't) you shall let it rest and lie fallow (tishmetennah u'netashtah): Shamat = to release/remit/let fall; natash = to let loose/abandon. The land gets rest — not merely a human agricultural technique but a theological statement: the land belongs to YHWH and must observe his rest (cf. Lev 25:2-4). That the poor of your people (aniyei ammecha) may eat: The fallow year's produce is freely available to the poor — the land's rest creates a welfare system for those who cannot purchase food. What the land produces without cultivation belongs to all.
12: Six days you shall do your work, but on the seventh day you shall rest (tishbot): Shavat = to cease/rest (the Sabbath verb from shabat); the Sabbath law restated in the social context of the Covenant Code — not just a creation ordinance but a social one. That your ox and your donkey may have rest, and the son of your servant woman and the alien (ger) may be refreshed (yinnafesh): Nafash = to take breath/be refreshed (from nefesh = breath/soul); even animals and resident aliens participate in the Sabbath rest. The Sabbath is the great equalizer: for one day, master and slave, citizen and alien, human and animal all rest together.
13: Pay attention to all that I have said to you, and make no mention of the names of other gods (shem elohim acherim): The law against invoking the names of foreign deities — shem (name) + zakar (to remember/mention). Names were not merely labels but invocations of power; to speak the name of a god was to invite its presence. The prohibition is reinforced: 'let it not be heard on your lips (lo yishama al-picha)' — the absolute exclusion of pagan invocation from Israelite speech.
14: Three times in the year (shalosh regalim) you shall keep a feast to me: Shalosh regalim = three foot-pilgrimages (regel = foot; the festivals were occasions for physical pilgrimage to the central sanctuary). The pilgrim-festival system is the organizing principle of Israel's annual liturgical calendar, integrating agricultural rhythms with redemptive history.
15: You shall keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread (chag hamatzot): The spring festival (month of Abib) commemorates the Exodus; seven days of matzot = the re-enacted experience of the departure. None shall appear before me empty-handed (lo-yeira'u fanai reqam): The Niphal of ra'ah (to be seen before) — pilgrimage to YHWH's presence requires bringing an offering; appearing empty-handed (reqam = empty) is not an option. The offering is the form of the encounter.
16: You shall keep the Feast of Harvest (chag hakatzir), of the firstfruits (bikkurim) of your labor: Katzir = harvest; bikkurim = firstfruits (from bakar, to be first); the late-spring grain festival, later called Shavuot (Weeks) or Pentecost (50 days from Passover). And the Feast of Ingathering (chag ha'asif) at the end of the year: Asif = gathering in; the autumn harvest festival (later called Sukkot = Booths), when all crops are brought in before the rains. The three festivals map the agricultural year (spring planting → early harvest → late harvest) onto redemptive commemoration.
17: Three times in the year all your males shall appear before the Lord YHWH (yira'eh kol-z'kurcha el-p'nei ha'adon YHWH): The triple pilgrimage requirement creates a national rhythm of covenant renewal; every adult male physically presents himself before YHWH's presence three times annually. The use of ha'adon (the Lord/Master) alongside YHWH is rare — the full divine title emphasizes the feudal-suzerain relationship of the Covenant.
18: You shall not offer the blood of my sacrifice with anything leavened (chametz): Sacrificial blood and leavened bread cannot occupy the same space — the unleavened purity of the Passover extends to all blood-sacrifice. The fat (chelev) of my feast shall not remain until morning: Chelev = the richest fat portions dedicated to YHWH; they must be consumed the same night — no overnight storage of the dedicated portions.
19: The best (reshit) of the firstfruits (bikkurei) of your ground you shall bring to the house of YHWH your God: Reshit = beginning/best/firstling; bikkurim = firstfruits; the double emphasis (best + first) maximizes the offering. You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk (lo tevashel gedi bachalav imo): The foundational prohibition of kashrut meat/milk separation; it appears three times in the Torah (here, Exod 34:26, Deut 14:21). The exact rationale is debated: Canaanite ritual practice? Compassion for the maternal bond? A symbolic refusal to use the life-giving substance (milk) to destroy life (boiling)? The Talmud extends it to complete separation of meat and dairy.
20: Behold, I send an angel (malach) before you to guard you on the way and to bring you to the place that I have prepared: Malach = messenger/angel (from the root for mission/commission); this is the mal'ach ha-panim (angel of the presence) — the divine emissary who accompanies Israel through the wilderness. Guard you on the way (lishmorcha baderech) and to bring you to the place: The angel serves as both protector and guide, performing the same functions as the pillar of cloud and fire.
21: Pay careful attention to him and obey his voice; do not rebel (al-tammer) against him: Marar = to be bitter/rebellious; the cognate of marah (the bitter waters). For he will not pardon your transgression (lo yissa l'pish'achem): Nasa' = to carry/bear/forgive; the angel will not bear/forgive rebellion because YHWH's name is in him (ki shmi bo = for my name is in him). The angel's authority is derivative — his name is YHWH's name; rebellion against the angel is rebellion against YHWH.
22: But if you carefully obey his voice and do all that I say, then I will be an enemy to your enemies (v'ayavti et-oyavecha) and an adversary to your adversaries (v'tzarti et-tzorecha): The covenant protective formula — YHWH mirrors Israel's conflict relationships. Oyev/tzar (enemy/adversary) are both political and spiritual terms; the protection covers all who oppose Israel's covenant fidelity.
23: When my angel goes before you and brings you to the Amorites and the Hittites and the Perizzites and the Canaanites, the Hivites and the Jebusites, and I blot them out (v'hichadtiv): Six-nation formula (vs. the usual seven or ten — variations signal different sources or rhetorical contexts); hachadah = to cut off/destroy. The extermination of the Canaanite nations is here presented as the direct act of YHWH through his angel — not Israelite military prowess but divine warfare.
24: You shall not bow down to their gods (lo tishtacheveh l'eloheihem) or serve them (lo ta'avdem): Shachah = to prostrate/bow in worship; abad = to serve/work; the two verbs of religious service are prohibited together. You shall utterly overthrow them (ki haros tiharesem) and break their pillars (v'shabeir t'shabeir matzevotam) in pieces: Haras = to tear down; shabar = to break/shatter; matzevah = standing stone/sacred pillar (the Canaanite cultic monuments that YHWH requires destroyed).
25: You shall serve (avadtem) YHWH your God, and he will bless your bread and your water: The exclusive service formula: avad YHWH (to serve/work for YHWH) is the positive counterpart to the prohibition of serving foreign gods. I will take sickness (machalah) away from among you: Machalah = disease/sickness (cf. 15:26 — YHWH the healer); the covenant blessing of health is the positive form of the disease-threat (Deut 28's curse list). Obedience → blessing of provision; disobedience → curse of disease.
26: None shall miscarry (m'shakkelet) or be barren (aqarah) in your land: Shakol = to miscarry/lose young (the verb of Rachel's fear and Jacob's grief — Gen 31:38, 43:14); aqarah = barren (the term for Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah — all whose barrenness precedes miraculous birth). The covenant blessing reverses the curse of infertility; covenant fidelity produces abundant offspring. I will fulfill the number of your days (et-mispar yamecha amaleh): Long life as covenant blessing — the years of one's life are in YHWH's hand to complete.
27: I will send my terror (emati) before you: Emah = terror/dread (the same word as the Song of the Sea, 15:16 — 'terror and dread fall upon them'); the divine terror-weapon precedes Israel's advance. I will throw into confusion (hamoti) all the people against whom you shall come: Hamam = to throw into confusion/panic (the same verb as 14:24 — YHWH throwing the Egyptian army into panic); divine warfare tactics: terror sent ahead, confusion at contact.
28: I will send hornets (tzirah) before you: Tzirah = hornet (an actual species, but also possibly a metaphor for terror-inducing panic or a supernatural agent); the word appears only here, Deut 7:20, and Josh 24:12. Whether literal insects or a metaphor for the demoralizing terror that drives nations into flight, the tzirah accomplishes what the human army alone cannot: pre-battlefield psychological collapse. Which shall drive out (v'gershah) the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite from before you: Garash = to drive out/expel (the verb of expulsion from Eden — Gen 3:24; and of Pharaoh expelling Moses — Exod 10:11).
29: I will not drive them out from before you in one year, lest the land become desolate (shamamah) and the wild beasts (chayat hasadeh) multiply against you: Shamamah = desolation/waste; the theological realism of the conquest — a sudden total expulsion would leave the land uninhabitable. YHWH's timing serves both Israel's security and the land's ecological stability. The text acknowledges the complexity of settling a land that has been cultivated by others: vacated land becomes wild.
30: Little by little (meat meat) I will drive them out from before you, until you have increased and possess the land: Meat meat = a little a little (the repeated diminutive for gradual process); the same principle applies to spiritual formation — YHWH works incrementally, matching the pace to the people's readiness. Until you have increased (parita) and possess the land: Parah = to be fruitful/multiply (the creation blessing verb of Gen 1:28); Israel's growth justifies the expansion of settlement.
31: I will set your border from the Red Sea to the Sea of the Philistines (Yam Pelishtim), and from the wilderness to the Euphrates (Nahar): The maximal promised land dimensions — from Yam Suph (Gulf of Aqaba) to the Mediterranean (Yam Pelishtim), from the Arabian desert to the Euphrates. This is the territorial ideal, partially realized under David and Solomon (1 Kgs 4:21). For I will give the inhabitants of the land into your hand: The divine land-gift is the basis for human military action; the conquest is YHWH's work, not Israel's.
32: You shall make no covenant (lo tichrot lahem brit) with them or with their gods: Karat brit = to cut a covenant (the technical term for covenant-making, from the cutting of the covenant sacrifice — Gen 15:18); the prohibition of covenant-making with Canaanites is comprehensive: no political treaty, no religious accommodation. The exclusive loyalty demanded by the covenant brooks no competing alliance structure.
33: They shall not dwell in your land, lest they make you sin against me (yachatiu otcha li): Chata = to miss the mark/sin; the resident Canaanites would serve as a mokesh (snare/trap — from yaqash, to trap birds) — a mechanism drawing Israel into their religious practices. The logic of the prohibition: tolerated idolatry becomes contagious. The mokesh (snare) image recurs in Deut 7:16, Judg 2:3 — its anticipation here proves accurate in the subsequent history.
